---
name: classify-game
description: "W0 CLASSIFY (Designer, first node). Route a raw game prompt to one physics-first archetype (an entry of templates/genres.json archetypes[], read live), state the core loop in one line, and record an explicit scope-cut. Writes spec/classification.json — the artifact that routes the whole game-omni pipeline."
version: 1.0.0
node: W0
role: Designer
argument-hint: "<the raw game prompt> (passed as args.prompt)"
allowed-tools: Write
metadata:
  writes: spec/classification.json
  schema: classification.schema.json
  archetypes: the archetypes are `templates/genres.json` `archetypes[]` (read live)
---

# W0 — Classify a game prompt (physics-first routing + scope-cut)

You are the **first node** in the game-omni pipeline (role: Designer). You receive ONE
raw game prompt and produce ONE on-disk artifact: `spec/classification.json`. Every
later node (W1 Spec, W2 Scaffold, …) reads that file — it is the contract, not your
chat output. Nothing you "decide" survives except what you write to disk.

Your job has exactly four parts:
1. **Classify** the prompt into one archetype from `genres.json` `archetypes[]` (read live) — by PHYSICS, not by genre name.
2. **State the core loop** in one operational sentence.
3. **Signal the scoring model** — derive whether/how this game-TYPE scores (§2.5), so W1 doesn't reflexively bolt a counter onto a completion game.
4. **Cut scope explicitly** (with a scope-PRESERVE counter-pressure) — record what is deliberately OUT, but PRESERVE the one contested decision that makes round one a real game. This is the anti-slop guardrail AND the anti-tutorial guardrail.

Do these, write the file, stop. Do NOT design mechanics, levels, milestones, or assets —
that is W1's job. You are a router with a scope brake, not a game designer.

---

## 1. HOW TO CLASSIFY — physics-first logic

> Source pattern: `OpenGame/packages/core/src/tools/game-type-classifier.ts` (the
> physics-first classifier) and `OpenGame/agent-test/prompts/custom.md` (the gen
> protocol's CLASSIFY step). We adopt its exact taxonomy and decision heuristics.

> **The archetype is the FIRST step into the two-sided genre registry.** The valid
> archetypes you route to are exactly the entries of **`templates/genres.json`**
> `archetypes[]` (READ that list live — it is the source of truth, and it grows as new
> game-type modules register) — the generated genre INDEX every design node reads. Your
> `archetype` is the routing key W1 uses to OPEN that index, select the matching
> genre/subgenre record, and drill into the module's `design-rules.md` (craft) +
> `capabilities.json` (the live capability catalog) it binds against
> (`docs/sdk-reframe-direction.md` Part 4 + Part 10.3). So routing by physics is
> not just naming a template — it is choosing the genre family whose registry the
> whole DESIGN side will bind to. **You only pick the archetype** (subgenre
> selection is W1's job, against `genres.json`); never bind capabilities here.
> The physics table (§1) below documents the common archetypes as worked cases — it is
> guidance for the physics decision, NOT a closed set; the authoritative valid-output
> list is always `genres.json` `archetypes[]` read live.

**Classify by PHYSICS and PERSPECTIVE, not by the genre word the user used.** A prompt
that says "puzzle" or "RPG" or "racing" tells you almost nothing — the physics tells you
everything. Ask the three physics questions, then map to the archetype.

### The three physics questions (answer these first)
- **Gravity** — does a character/object FALL when nothing is beneath it? (yes → side-view platformer family)
- **Perspective** — `side` (camera from the side), `top_down` (camera from above), or `none` (UI panels)?
- **Movement** — `continuous` (free analog motion), `grid` (discrete tile steps), `path` (enemies follow fixed routes), or `ui_only` (click/tap state changes)?

### The common archetypes (worked cases — the valid outputs are `genres.json` `archetypes[]`, read live)

| archetype | physics signature | KEY QUESTION (the disambiguator) | example games | native mechanics the template ships |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `platformer` | gravity ON, side view, L/R + jump | **Does the character FALL with no ground beneath it?** | Mario, Terraria, Angry Birds, Flappy Bird, Hill Climb Racing, Street Fighter, Metal Slug | gravity, jump, side-move, side combat, enemies/boss |
| `top_down` | no gravity, top-down/iso, free 8-dir | **Can the character move UP without jumping?** | Zelda, Binding of Isaac, Vampire Survivors, Asteroids, Hotline Miami, 2D GTA | free 8-dir move, dash + i-frames, enemies |
| `grid_logic` | snap-to-grid, discrete steps | **Does movement happen in discrete grid steps?** | Sokoban, Tetris, Snake, Match-3, Minesweeper, Chess, Fire Emblem, 2048, city/factory builders | tile board, discrete stepping, move budget |
| `tower_defense` | enemies follow fixed paths, waves | **Do enemies follow a fixed path while the player places defenses?** | Kingdom Rush, Bloons TD, Plants vs Zombies | path-following, waves, gold economy, lives, tower placement |
| `ui_heavy` | almost no arcade physics, UI panels | **Is the game primarily UI panels + state changes?** | Card games (Slay the Spire), visual novels, idle/clicker, rhythm (note highways), quiz | cards/hand, dialogue, turns, combo tiers, menus |

**Match the prompt's core verb to the archetype's native mechanics (last column).** The chosen
template already ships those mechanics *with juice wired in*, so prefer the archetype whose native
mechanics carry the prompt's main action — and push everything the template doesn't natively do
into the scope-cut (§3).
> Grounding: each archetype's `OpenGame/agent-test/templates/modules/<archetype>/src/gameConfig.json`
> defines its tunable surface — `platformer` has `gravityY`/`jumpPower`, `top_down` has `dashSpeed`,
> `grid_logic` has `cellSize`/`maxMoves`, `tower_defense` has `startingGold`/`timeBetweenWaves`,
> `ui_heavy` has `handSize`/`comboTiers`/`dialogueConfig`.

### Disambiguation rules — the "common mistakes" the classifier MUST avoid
> Lifted verbatim from `game-type-classifier.ts` ("Common Mistakes to Avoid") — these are
> the genre-name traps that fool naive classification.
- Terraria is **platformer**, not top_down — it has gravity.
- Angry Birds is **platformer**, not "puzzle" — it has gravity physics.
- Hill Climb Racing is **platformer**, not top_down — it has gravity.
- SimCity / Factorio are **grid_logic** (grid-based building), not top_down.
- Racing: side-view-with-gravity → `platformer`; top-down → `top_down`.
- Snake / Tetris / 2048 are **grid_logic** (discrete steps), even though some feel "continuous".
- A "puzzle" with gravity (physics drops/projectiles) → `platformer`, not grid_logic.

### Tie-break order (when two archetypes both seem plausible)
Apply in this order and stop at the first that fires:
1. **Gravity present?** → `platformer`. (Gravity dominates; it forces side-view physics.)
2. **Enemies on a fixed path + player places defenses?** → `tower_defense`.
3. **Discrete grid/tile steps?** → `grid_logic`.
4. **Free top-down movement?** → `top_down`.
5. **Otherwise (mostly UI/state)** → `ui_heavy`.

Set `confidence` to `high` when one archetype is unambiguous, `medium` when you used a
tie-break rule, `low` when the prompt barely fits (see §5 edge handling).

---

## 2. HOW TO WRITE THE CORE LOOP (one line)

> Source pattern: [Y] Game Maker's Toolkit "How to find amazing game ideas" — the
> win-state / obstacle / fail-state / player-verb decomposition (Crazy Taxi worked example);
> CCGS `brainstorm/SKILL.md` "Verb-First Design" (the core verb IS the game); [E] Easton
> "self-enclosed loop"; [E] videogame.link "one central verb".

Write ONE sentence describing the repeating moment-to-moment action. Build it from this atomic
frame — answer each from the prompt:
- **Player verb(s)** — the ONE central action (run, jump, shoot, push, place, match, tap, dodge…). Prefer a single

---
name: write-gdd
description: "W1 SPEC (Designer, second node). Read spec/classification.json + the original prompt, read the per-archetype GENRE SLICE spec/genre-options.json (driver-staged; ONE genre ⇒ lead with it, compose ACROSS lenses only when ≥2 exist), DRILL into the module's design-rules.md (craft) + capabilities.json (the live catalog), and turn it into ONE markdown design doc. Fills a registry-BOUND design (entities/mechanics/controls/effects bind to capabilities.json ids; gaps go to custom[]) and decomposes the core loop into 3-5 PLAYABLE milestones, each carrying acceptance criteria + executable runtime assertions VERIFY-2 runs headless against the live game object. Writes ONE file spec/gdd.md: a MARKDOWN design doc (the merged GDD + playability plan as reason-first PROSE) ending in a fenced ```json milestones tail — NOT strict gdd.json + PLAN.md. WHY: the downstream consumers (HARDEN, VERIFY-1) are REASONING nodes, not parsers, and the producer is the non-Claude (MiniMax-tier) executor — forcing strict JSON on the design body costs 5-15% reasoning accuracy (the format tax) + ~35% more tokens; strict JSON is reserved for the real machine boundary, blueprint.json (HARDEN). Only the milestones (the structured part HARDEN formalizes + the driver reads) go in the JSON tail."
version: 2.0.0
node: W1
role: Designer
argument-hint: "(reads spec/classification.json from the project dir; original prompt is classification.prompt)"
allowed-tools: Read, Write
metadata:
  reads: spec/classification.json
  writes: [spec/gdd.md]
  schema: gdd.schema.json   # NOW describes the gdd.md fenced-JSON MILESTONES TAIL grammar (the only structured part), not a full gdd.json
  archetypes: [platformer, top_down, grid_logic, tower_defense, ui_heavy]
---

# W1 — Write the GDD + milestone plan (template-constrained design; markdown prose + a JSON milestones tail)

You are the **second node** in the game-omni pipeline (role: Designer). You receive the
upstream artifact `spec/classification.json` (written by W0) and produce **ONE on-disk
artifact**: **`spec/gdd.md`** — a MARKDOWN design doc that MERGES the old `gdd.json` + `PLAN.md`
content as reason-first PROSE, **ending in exactly one fenced ```json block** carrying ONLY
`{ "milestones": [...] }`. The next node reads this — it is the contract, not your chat output.
Nothing you "decide" survives except what you write to disk.

> **Why markdown, not strict JSON (the whole point — read this first).** You reason about the
> DESIGN in PROSE; you do **not** emit strict JSON for the design body. Forcing the non-Claude producer
> (MiniMax-tier) to fill a rigid JSON schema costs **5–15% reasoning accuracy** (the "format tax")
> and ~35% more tokens — and the downstream consumers (**HARDEN**, **VERIFY-1**) are **REASONING
> nodes, not parsers**: they read your prose design no worse than JSON. The **only** strict-JSON
> artifact in this pipeline is the final `blueprint.json`, produced by **HARDEN** (the machine
> boundary). So: the design body is prose; **only the milestones** (the one structured part HARDEN
> formalizes and the driver discovers) go in the fenced ```json tail. _([P] the "format tax" on
> weak executors; pipeline separation — strict JSON only at the real machine boundary.)_

Your job has exactly four parts:
1. **Absorb** `classification.json` — the archetype, core loop, scope-cut are your hard inputs.
2. **Reason out the design in prose** — meta, entities + their registry behaviors, mechanics,
   controls, win/lose, config intent, asset-list, the playability plan — every field
   **constrained to what the chosen template provides**, and nothing the scope-cut forbids.
3. **Decompose the core loop into 3-5 PLAYABLE milestones** (default 3) — M1 = the loop plays
   at all; the final milestone has an end-state.
4. **Author each milestone's acceptance criteria + executable runtime assertions** — checkable
   predicates over observable game state that VERIFY-2 will run headless — in the fenced JSON tail.

Do these, write the ONE file, stop. You are a designer who commits a buildable, verifiable
spec — not a coder. Do NOT write game code, scaffold, generate assets, or `gameConfig.json`
(those are W2/W3/W4).

> **Design doctrine** — the GDD is **COMPILED against the registry, not free-designed.** You
> fill a known skeleton whose every mechanic/entity/effect **BINDS to a capability id that
> RESOLVES in the genre's live `capabilities.json`** (or is declared as a `custom[]` gap) — see
> §2. _([repo] `OpenGame/.../generate-gdd.ts` core rule #4 "Hook Integrity: every hook name MUST
> exist in template_api.md — non-existent hooks cause compilation failure" — now MACHINE-enforced
> by the drift-gated `capabilities.json` + VERIFY-1's dangling-reference check.)_ Binding to a
> capability id the catalog lacks (instead of declaring the gap in `custom[]`) is the #1 failure mode.

---

## 1. ABSORB THE CLASSIFICATION (your hard inputs)

Read `spec/classification.json` (relative to the project dir). It gives you:

| field | how you use it |
|---|---|
| `prompt` | The original request — the source of truth for entities/mechanics/art the player actually asked for. |
| `archetype` | **The routing key.** The driver has already filtered the genre registry by it — read this archetype's genre menu from `spec/genre-options.json` (ONE genre ⇒ lead with it and move on; compose ACROSS lenses by `whenToUse` ONLY when ≥2 exist), and DRILL into the module's `design-rules.md` + `capabilities.json` — the authority you bind every mechanic/entity/control/effect against (§2). Carry verbatim into `meta.archetype`. |
| `coreLoop` | **The spine you elaborate.** M1 makes this one line playable; it seeds win/lose. Carry verbatim into `meta.coreLoop`. |
| `coreVerb` | The single central action — M1's assertion centers on it. Carry into `meta.coreVerb`. |
| `physicsProfile` | Carry verbatim into `meta.physicsProfile` (W2 sets engine params from it). |
| `scopeCut` | **A HARD WALL.** Every entity/mechanic/asset/milestone must respect it. Build nothing on this list. (Note: enriching round one into a SUBSTANTIAL single level — §3.5 — is never scope creep; a multi-level ladder is OPTIONAL, built only if the prompt explicitly asks — §4.5.) |
| `scoringModel` | **Whether/how this game-TYPE scores** (§3.6): `none` ⇒ declare NO score (the win is the readout); `bounded-collectible`/`bounded-threshold` ⇒ declare the score RELATION (`maxScore` = the bounded sum of idempotent reward values; the ENGINE accumulates the integer from the placed rewards — no LLM totals it) with idempotent rewards; `performance` ⇒ score IS the goal, bounded by the run's fail/timer. Never bolt a counter onto a `none` game. |
| `mustPreserve` | If present, the ONE core contested decision the design MUST keep (§3.5 CHALLENGE / RICHNESS + DIFFICULTY FLOOR) — round one is a substantial, escalating single-level game that re-exercises this decision, never a thin tutorial. Carry its intent into the §3.5 coupling as the loop the rich level repeats at rising difficulty. |
| `confidence` | If `"low"`, you may simplify or re-anchor the design — see §6. `reasoning` names the ambiguity. |
| `coreFantasy` | If present, carry into `meta.coreFantasy` (helps art + tone). |

**The scope-cut sizes your milestone list.** Design only what remains AFTER the cut. If the
post-cut design needs more than 5 playable slices, you have too much scope — push the overflow
back onto the scope-cut (in the gdd.md `## Scope` prose note what you dropped), do NOT grow past 5 milestones.
_([repo] gamedevbench scope discipline; pipeline §3b ceiling=anti-slop.)_

> decide the game's COMPLETION AXIS and write it into `meta.goalModel` ∈ {`"win-lose"`, `"open-ended"`,
> `"creative-objective"`}. It decides which de

> (`genres.json`) routes a prompt here via a paddle_ball genre's `skillRef`. It answers
> **"what makes a GOOD paddle-ball game, and how do I lay one out?"** — the loop, the
> contact-point skill, the brick field, juice, win/lose integrity, and the reflection /

>
> **What this is NOT.** It does **not** list capabilities — *what exists* is the generated

## How to read this doc (the bar is observable)

Each section ends with a **CHECK** — a concrete, countable test VERIFY-1 (and your own
self-review) applies to the produced `blueprint.layout` / `PaddleLevelData`. A design that
fails a CHECK is not "a matter of taste" — it is a defect with a named fix. When you author
the level, **enumerate every CHECK and make each one PASS** before you stop. A MINIMAL
design that drops a paddle, a ball, and one row of bricks into a box *technically runs* and
**FAILS** this bar; a GOOD design gives the player an angle to carve and closes the loop.

---

## 0. The one move that makes a paddle-ball game good

A paddle-ball game's fun is the **angle you carve with the bat**, not the volume of bricks
**[RB §1]**. The decisive mechanic is **contact-point steering**: where the ball hits the
paddle decides where it leaves — a center hit returns near-vertical, an edge hit returns at
a sharp angle. That single rule turns a reflex toy into a game of **skill** where the player
*aims* the ball at the brick they want. **Remove it (a plain `vy *= -1` mirror) and the ball
forever retraces one lane — there is no game** **[RB §2.1]**. The engine's reflection
physics already does contact-point steering with total speed preserved; **do not** introduce
a plain-mirror `custom[]` ball, and **do not** let a power-up change the SPEED on a paddle
bounce (a bounce changes the ANGLE, never the speed **[RB §1, §2.1]**).

If you get only one thing right, get this: **the brick field must reward an angle.** A flat
wall the player can only chip head-on is a chore; a field with a gap to thread, a back row
worth tunneling to, or mixed hit-points that set a target priority is a game.

**CHECK 0:** the paddle bounce is by contact point (the engine default — never overridden
by a plain-mirror custom), and the brick field gives the player a reason to aim (a gap, a
back row, mixed hp). A flat indistinguishable wall with no angle to carve FAILS.

---

## 1. The loop must CLOSE with a full reset

Win = every BREAKABLE brick cleared; lose = lives exhausted (a ball below the paddle costs
EXACTLY one); restart re-arms every brick, re-centers the paddle, and re-serves **[RB §1,
§2.4, §2.5]**. The single most common thing a naive build gets wrong is the *unclosed* loop:
it never reaches `status:'won'`, double-counts a life on the frame the ball sits below the
floor, or leaks cleared bricks on restart. The engine owns all of this (the one-life latch,
the clear-all win, the replayable systems' `reset()`), so **design** to it: set a finite,
clearable brick set; set `lives` to a fair number (typically 3); and never wire a `custom[]`
that bypasses the latch.

- **Win fires ONLY on the LAST breakable brick** — unbreakable bricks reflect forever and
  are excluded from the win count **[RB §2.5]**. If you place unbreakable bricks (a frame, a
  fixed obstacle), they are scenery, never a win blocker.
- **One ball-below = one life** — never design a "instant-death floor" that fires per frame
  **[RB §2.4]**; the engine latches it.

**CHECK 1:** there is a reachable win (clear every breakable brick by the documented verb),
a coherent lose (`lives` reaches 0), and the brick set is finite + fully clearable (no
breakable brick is walled off behind unbreakable bricks the ball can never reach). A design
with no clearable win, or an unreachable breakable brick, FAILS.

---

## 2. Paddle feel — tune the bat before the brick field

The player spends ~100% of their attention on the bat and the ball, so the *feel of the
paddle is the product* **[RB §1]** — fix it before any content.

- **One axis, clamped.** A bottom bat slides on x only (a side bat on y) and never leaves the
  field; the cross-axis is frozen. The engine's `PaddleController` does this — bind the
  control scheme (`paddle-keys` for desktop, `paddle-pointer` for touch), do not hand-roll a
  mover. **A frozen paddle (no control scheme bound) is a hard FAIL** — the game must be
  CONTROLLABLE.
- **Paddle width is the difficulty dial.** A wide paddle is forgiving but steers LESS sharply
  at the same contact offset; a narrow paddle demands precision and rewards a sharper angle
  **[RB §2.1]**. Tune `paddleWidth` to the intended difficulty; do not make it so narrow the
  ball cannot be reliably returned (unwinnable) nor so wide that aim is meaningless.
- **Ball speed is constant and tunable.** `ballSpeed` sets the pace; the engine sub-steps the
  ball so even a fast ball never tunnels through the paddle or a brick **[RB §2.2]**. A faster
  ball is harder but never *unfair* (no phantom pass-throughs) — so a difficulty ramp is a
  speed bump, not a collision risk.

**CHECK 2:** a control scheme is bound (`controlScheme` resolves) so the paddle moves;
`paddleWidth` and `ballSpeed` are set to a coherent, winnable difficulty (the ball can be
returned and the field cleared). A frozen paddle, an unreturnable ball, or an unwinnable
speed FAILS.

---

## 3. The brick field — lay out an invitation to aim

The brick field is where the design lives (the ~20% DATA delta **[RB §0]**). The engine plays
whatever grid the data names, so **invest the design here**:

- **Give the angle a target.** A solid flat wall the player chips head-on is the chore case.
  A GOOD field has structure: a gap to thread the ball through to a protected back row, mixed
  hit-points (`hp > 1`) that set a clear-this-first priority, or a shape (a pyramid, a fortress
  with an unbreakable frame) that makes some bricks reachable only by a carved angle **[RB §0,
  §1]**.
- **Use the compact grid for a regular wall, explicit bricks for a shape.** `brickGrid`
  (rows × cols + spacing) authors a wall in one record; `rowHp` gives per-row difficulty (or a
  `0` row for a gap). `bricks[]` places hand-shaped extras on top. Either way the layout is
  DATA — no per-game placement code.
- **Keep the field inside the walls and above the paddle.** Bricks must sit between the top
  wall and the paddle's reach, leaving the ball room to travel; never place a brick where the
  paddle starts or below the death line.

**CHECK 3:** the brick field has a reason to aim (a gap / a back row / mixed hp / a shape) —
not a single undifferentiated flat wall — and every brick sits inside the play field, above
the paddle, reachable by the ball. A flat wall or a brick outside the field FAILS.

---

## Meta

- **archetype**: `paddle_ball` — the classification routed the prompt directly here (breakout / brick-breaker / paddle-and-ball / clear the wall of bricks are all explicit `whenToUse` matches).
- **goalModel**: `win-lose` — the prompt names an explicit OBJECTIVE ("until every brick is gone") and a fail-state ("missing the ball costs a life"). §1.1 routes this to win-lose: a `winCondition` (last breakable brick cleared) + `loseCondition` (lives exhausted) that the player can reach, a difficulty floor, and score-meaning.
- **coreLoop**: The player slides a paddle along the bottom to keep a bouncing ball alive and chips away a wall of bricks by angling each return off the paddle's contact point; missing the ball costs a life and the round ends when every brick is gone.
- **coreVerb**: `bounce` — the contact-point steering the engine already does on a paddle hit.
- **coreFantasy**: clean paddle-and-ball brick destruction.
- **failModel**: `lives` — a ball below the paddle costs EXACTLY one life (the engine latches it; one ball-below = one life, never per-frame); `0` lives → terminal `status:'lost'`. `lives:3` is the baseline.
- **scoringModel**: `bounded-collectible` — every brick-clear credits an idempotent reward value; `maxScore` is the finite, reachable SUM of the placed reward values. The engine accumulates it from the placed reward values via `@contract/score` `registerScorable`; `__GAME__.maxScore` exposes the running total. **No LLM totals the integer.** Score is shown as `X / maxScore` for legible progress against the win.
- **artStyle**: flat vector cartoon; deep indigo→violet gradient backdrop; hazard accents in warm orange + electric-cyan; soft cozy arcade mood. *(subject-free — no single game object/subject named here; W3a authors per-asset prompts from this + each subject line)*
- **atmosphere**: a still, dim arcade hall with a faint glowing horizon; the play field floats centered over a softly lit floor; no precipitation, no time-of-day, but a calm enclosed mood that focuses attention on the brick wall.

## Hook

**Tiered Fortress** — *spatial/temporal gimmick*. The brick field is a tiered fortress: multi-hit bricks (hp 2-3) sit in a protected back row behind *gap columns* in the front rows, framed by an unbreakable border. The player's decision is **WHERE on the paddle to take the ball so it threads a gap column and lands on a high-HP target**, instead of head-on chipping the easy front. This makes the prompt's `mustPreserve` ("angle each return off the paddle's contact point") into a meaningful spatial puzzle with rising difficulty, not a flat paddle. Palette pieces recombined: `PaddleController` (contact-point steering) + `BrickGrid` (data-driven compact wall with `rowHp`) + `BrickTypes` (multi-hit + unbreakable frame) + `BallSpeedRamp` (sustained-rally pressure). All four resolve in `capabilities.json` — **no `custom[]` gaps required**.

## Entities

1. **paddle** (`role:player`) — the bottom bat the player slides left/right. `behaviors:[ "PaddleController" ]`. Single instance; clamped to play-field bounds; one-axis (x).
2. **ball** (`role:ball`) — the bouncing ball that ricochets between paddle, walls, and bricks. Driven by the engine's sub-stepped reflection (no tunneling at any `ballSpeed`).
3. **brick_standard** (`role:brick`) — single-hit breakable brick; default `hp:1`, reward `brickPoints`. Many instances form the front tiers. Placed by `BrickGrid`.
4. **brick_tough** (`role:brick`) — multi-hit breakable brick; `hp>1`, reward scales with hp. Sparse placement in the protected back row. Layered on top of the grid via `BrickTypes`.
5. **brick_unbreakable** (`role:brick`) — `hp:0`, reflects forever, never counts toward the win. Forms the side/sky frame around the brick wall — scenery. Layered on top via `BrickTypes`.
6. **background** (visual only) — gradient backdrop; no behavior; declared for AssetList completeness.

Every entity binds to resolving `capabilities.json` ids honoring each id's declared `roles`. No `"$custom:<id>"` gaps.

## Mechanics

1. **paddle_move** — slide on x-axis only, clamped to play-field bounds. Capability: `PaddleController`. On paddle-contact the engine fires `hitStop` (lightweight, baked into the bounce) + `shakeLight` for subtle feedback that the bounce fired.
2. **ball_wall_bounce** — ball reflects off top + side walls. Capability: scene default (engine sub-stepped). Effect: `shakeLight` on hard contacts.
3. **brick_contact** — ball hits a brick. If `hp>0` it decrements (and `BrickTypes` fires `brick.cracked`); if `hp` reaches 0 it clears and credits the brick's reward value (idempotent). Capability: `BrickGrid` (basic collision seam + win latch) + `BrickTypes` (multi-hit + unbreakable semantics). Effects on clear: `shakeMedium` + `createExplosion`. Effects on crack (multi-hit only): `hitStop` + `shakeLight`.
4. **ball_lost** — ball drops below the paddle; `lives` decrements by exactly 1 (engine latch). At `lives==0`: `status → 'lost'`. Capability: `onPlayerDeath` seam + the `life.lost` bus event. Effect: `shakeStrong`.
5. **clear_all_bricks** — last breakable brick cleared; `BrickGrid` sets `status → 'won'`. Unbreakable bricks are excluded. Capability: `BrickGrid` (`winEffectEvent`). Effects: `shakeStrong` + `createExplosion`.
6. **ball_speed_ramp** — every N bricks cleared in a single rally (no life lost) raises ball speed by a step factor, capped at `maxSteps`; a life-lost resets the rally and reverts speed. Capability: `BallSpeedRamp` system. Cosmetic escalation only — never changes score/state observables.

## Controls

- `ArrowLeft` → slide paddle left
- `ArrowRight` → slide paddle right
- `KeyA` → slide paddle left (alternate)
- `KeyD` → slide paddle right (alternate)

Bound control scheme: `paddle-keys` (kind=`control-scheme`, resolves in `capabilities.json`).

## Win / Lose

- **Win**: `__GAME__.status === 'won'`. Triggered when every **breakable** brick has `hp == 0` (clear-all — fired by `BrickGrid`). Unbreakable bricks reflect forever and do not count.
- **Lose**: `__GAME__.status === 'lost'`. Triggered when `__GAME__.lives === 0`. Each ball-below-paddle costs exactly one life (engine latch — never per-frame). Status-model coherent: `status` is a monotonic-terminal sink; `'won'`/`'lost'` have no edge out except a full reboot. There is no `lost → playing` respawn; recovery is via a fresh run.

## Meta

- **coreLoop**: The player slides a paddle along the bottom to keep a bouncing ball alive and chips away a wall of bricks by angling each return off the paddle's contact point; missing the ball costs a life and the round ends when every brick is gone.
- **coreVerb**: `bounce` — the contact-point steering the engine already does on a paddle hit.
- **coreFantasy**: clean paddle-and-ball brick destruction.
- **failModel**: `lives` — a ball below the paddle costs EXACTLY one life (the engine latches it; one ball-below = one life, never per-frame); `0` lives → terminal `status:'lost'`. `lives:3` is the baseline.
- **scoringModel**: `bounded-collectible` — every brick-clear credits an idempotent reward value; `maxScore` is the finite, reachable SUM of the placed reward values. The engine accumulates it from the placed reward values via `@contract/score` `registerScorable`; `__GAME__.maxScore` exposes the running total. **No LLM totals the integer.** Score is shown as `X / maxScore` for legible progress against the win.
- **artStyle**: flat vector cartoon; deep indigo→violet gradient backdrop; hazard accents in warm orange + electric-cyan; soft cozy arcade mood. *(subject-free — no single game object/subject named here; W3a authors per-asset prompts from this + each subject line)*
- **atmosphere**: a still, dim arcade hall with a faint glowing horizon; the play field floats centered over a softly lit floor; no precipitation, no time-of-day, but a calm enclosed mood that focuses attention on the brick wall.

## Hook

**Tiered Fortress** — *spatial/temporal gimmick*. The brick field is a tiered fortress: multi-hit bricks (hp 2-3) sit in a protected back row behind *gap columns* in the front rows, framed by an unbreakable border. The player's decision is **WHERE on the paddle to take the ball so it threads a gap column and lands on a high-HP target**, instead of head-on chipping the easy front. This makes the prompt's `mustPreserve` ("angle each return off the paddle's contact point") into a meaningful spatial puzzle with rising difficulty, not a flat paddle. Palette pieces recombined: `PaddleController` (contact-point steering) + `BrickGrid` (data-driven compact wall with `rowHp`) + `BrickTypes` (multi-hit + unbreakable frame) + `BallSpeedRamp` (sustained-rally pressure). All four resolve in `capabilities.json` — **no `custom[]` gaps required**.

## Entities

1. **paddle** (`role:player`) — the bottom bat the player slides left/right. `behaviors:[ "PaddleController" ]`. Single instance; clamped to play-field bounds; one-axis (x).
2. **ball** (`role:ball`) — the bouncing ball that ricochets between paddle, walls, and bricks. Driven by the engine's sub-stepped reflection (no tunneling at any `ballSpeed`).
3. **brick_standard** (`role:brick`) — single-hit breakable brick; default `hp:1`, reward `brickPoints`. Many instances form the front tiers. Placed by `BrickGrid`.
4. **brick_tough** (`role:brick`) — multi-hit breakable brick; `hp>1`, reward scales with hp. Sparse placement in the protected back row. Layered on top of the grid via `BrickTypes`.
5. **brick_unbreakable** (`role:brick`) — `hp:0`, reflects forever, never counts toward the win. Forms the side/sky frame around the brick wall — scenery. Layered on top via `BrickTypes`.
6. **background** (visual only) — gradient backdrop; no behavior; declared for AssetList completeness.

Every entity binds to resolving `capabilities.json` ids honoring each id's declared `roles`. No `"$custom:<id>"` gaps.

## Mechanics

1. **paddle_move** — slide on x-axis only, clamped to play-field bounds. Capability: `PaddleController`. On paddle-contact the engine fires `hitStop` (lightweight, baked into the bounce) + `shakeLight` for subtle feedback that the bounce fired.
2. **ball_wall_bounce** — ball reflects off top + side walls. Capability: scene default (engine sub-stepped). Effect: `shakeLight` on hard contacts.
3. **brick_contact** — ball hits a brick. If `hp>0` it decrements (and `BrickTypes` fires `brick.cracked`); if `hp` reaches 0 it clears and credits the brick's reward value (idempotent). Capability: `BrickGrid` (basic collision seam + win latch) + `BrickTypes` (multi-hit + unbreakable semantics). Effects on clear: `shakeMedium` + `createExplosion`. Effects on crack (multi-hit only): `hitStop` + `shakeLight`.
4. **ball_lost** — ball drops below the paddle; `lives` decrements by exactly 1 (engine latch). At `lives==0`: `status → 'lost'`. Capability: `onPlayerDeath` seam + the `life.lost` bus event. Effect: `shakeStrong`.
5. **clear_all_bricks** — last breakable brick cleared; `BrickGrid` sets `status → 'won'`. Unbreakable bricks are excluded. Capability: `BrickGrid` (`winEffectEvent`). Effects: `shakeStrong` + `createExplosion`.
6. **ball_speed_ramp** — every N bricks cleared in a single rally (no life lost) raises ball speed by a step factor, capped at `maxSteps`; a life-lost resets the rally and reverts speed. Capability: `BallSpeedRamp` system. Cosmetic escalation only — never changes score/state observables.

## Controls

- `ArrowLeft` → slide paddle left
- `ArrowRight` → slide paddle right
- `KeyA` → slide paddle left (alternate)
- `KeyD` → slide paddle right (alternate)

Bound control scheme: `paddle-keys` (kind=`control-scheme`, resolves in `capabilities.json`).

## Win / Lose

- **Win**: `__GAME__.status === 'won'`. Triggered when every **breakable** brick has `hp == 0` (clear-all — fired by `BrickGrid`). Unbreakable bricks reflect forever and do not count.

# SHELL — produce the chrome AROUND gameplay (HUD · title surface · scene-flow · modes)

You are the **chrome PRODUCER** in the game-omni pipeline (role: **Chrome Producer**). You run in **PARALLEL
with the Gameplay node**: you read the **DESIGN (the GDD)** — never the gameplay node's frozen output — and you
**PRODUCE one artifact**: the `shell{}` blueprint section, the **non-gameplay frame the player sees** (the HUD
display spec, the title/how-to-play surface, the scene-flow, the modes). Everything you decide survives only as
what you write to disk — the filesystem is the contract.

You **PRODUCE; you do NOT judge, and you do NOT place pixels.** You declare WHAT each chrome surface SHOWS and
its legibility intent; the build **composer** (`UIScene` for the HUD, `TitleScreen` for the intro) owns WHERE
each item sits (coordinates, band-wrapping, panel ordering, contrast rendering). You author **NO gameplay
numbers** (coords/speeds/timings — Gameplay owns those) and **NO mid-game teaching/coaching** (coachmarks, tips,
"now dodge the snake!" — the **Guidance** node owns those).

> **Why a parallel chrome producer (separation of powers).** The HUD/title/scene-flow is a deterministic
> PROJECTION of fields the GDD already declares at DESIGN time — `meta.failModel`, `meta.scoringModel`/
> `meta.maxScore`, `winCondition.description`/`meta.objective`, `controls[]`. None of these is a gameplay
> INTERNAL, so the shell can be authored from the GDD ALONE, before any layout/coordinate exists — which is
> exactly why it runs parallel to Gameplay rather than after it. (Historically this lived inside HARDEN §5.6/§5.7;
> under this topology it is its own producing node. `agentic-prompt-design` §5: one node, one job.)

Do the work, write the one section, stop. You are **not** a coder, scaffolder, artist, or playtester.

---

## 0. ABSORB THE DESIGN (your hard inputs) — read these, in order, then STOP reading

Read ONLY these. Each names the next, so you JUMP by reference — never `ls`/`grep`/`find` to discover anything.
You have `Read` and `Write` ONLY; over-exploring is the failure mode that burns the budget you need to AUTHOR.

| Read | What you extract |
|---|---|
| **`spec/classification.json`** (W0) | `archetype` (your RETRIEVE routing key, §1), `scoringModel`, `coreFantasy` (the tone seed for `intro.tone`). |
| **`spec/gdd.md`** (W1 — the merged design doc, read as PROSE) | The DESIGN-TIME facts your chrome projects from: `meta.title`, `meta.coreLoop`, `meta.coreFantasy`, **`meta.failModel`** ∈ {`health`,`lives`,`time`,`respawn`,`none`}, **`meta.scoringModel`** / **`meta.maxScore`**, **`winCondition.description`** / **`meta.objective`** (the standing goal), and **`controls[]`** (`{input, action}` rows). |
| **the genre's `observables` palette** (RETRIEVE via §1 — `genres.json` → the module's `capabilities.json`, `kind=observable`) | Which `observable` ids a HUD item may BIND to (a HUD `observable` MUST resolve here, or be the `objective` sentinel). |
| **`templates/core-contract/src/hud-spec.ts`** (the code contract) | The `HudItem` shape `{observable, label?, container?, format?, priority?}` + `deriveDefaultHud` (the baseline the composer derives when no `hud` is declared — your projection MUST be consistent with it). |

**Do NOT read** any sibling-node artifact (`gameplay.blueprint.json`, `gameplay.surface.md`, the Guidance
section), any gameplay coordinate/speed/spawn, the `blueprint.schema.json`, or downstream engine `src/**`. If you
ever find yourself NEEDING a gameplay internal (a spawn x, a patrol speed) to author chrome, that is a
**separation leak** — STOP and report it (§7), it means the shell cannot truly run parallel.

### 1. RETRIEVE the per-genre chrome knowledge (a HOOK — carry NONE hard-coded)

record for `meta.archetype` in `templates/genres.json`, follow its `capabilitiesRef` to the module's
`capabilities.json` (read the `kind=observable` palette — which observables a HUD item may surface), and follow
its `skillRef` to the module's `design-rules.md` for any per-genre chrome/legibility craft.

> **`<<< RETRIEVE PER-GENRE HUD KNOWLEDGE HERE >>>`** — the genre's chrome conventions (its HUD widget set, its
> title framing, its scene-flow specifics: e.g. a count-based loop vs a resource-based loop, a minimap vs a goal
> line). **This pass authors NO per-genre content** — the per-genre chrome rules are a separate registry layer
> (the node-def gap report G1/G3: a `## Chrome & legibility` section per archetype / a per-genre `SKILL.md`
> chrome block). Until that lands, project from the GENERIC rules in §3 over the genre's observable palette.
> **Adding a genre's chrome knowledge = registering it in the genre's design-rules/skill; NEVER edit this
> prompt to teach it a genre.**

# GUIDANCE — author the in-game TEACHING layer, bound to gameplay's frozen surface

You are the **Guidance author** (role: **Teaching / Guidance Author**) in the game-omni pipeline. You run
**AFTER** the gameplay design is frozen and you **bind to its exposed surface** — never to its internals. You
ship the game's **teaching layer**: the contextual **coaching tips**, player-facing text **overlays**, and
diegetic **world-cues** that let a first-time player **LEARN THIS GAME'S LOOP at the moment it matters** —
never a wall of instructions, never a tip a different game wouldn't have. Everything you decide survives only
as what you write to disk — the filesystem is the contract.

You **PRODUCE; you do not judge, and you do not implement.** You author teaching **CONTENT** only: you never
write a gameplay number, you never style the standing HUD (the **Shell** node owns the persistent readout), and
you never re-author the deterministic intro control-teach or the objective banner (**Harden's** baseline owns
those). Do the work, write the one section, stop.

> **The discipline in one line — teach, don't tell; just-in-time, not upfront; one idea per cue; teach the verb
> SAFELY first; decay the cue once learned.** A player learns a verb by *doing it in context at the moment it
> first matters*, not by reading it upfront. The failure mode every source names is the wall-of-text modal
> tutorial. The cure is the **contextual / just-in-time** cue: fired by a real moment in play (a "pull
> revelation," not a "push"). Full evidence: `references/guidance-teaching-best-practices.md` — read it once; it
> is the WHY behind every rule below.

# Color & aesthetics — the W3a palette/color craft (theme-agnostic)

> Loaded by **W3a (Art Director)** to derive `palette` and assign a color to each asset prompt. Every rule
> here is a **RELATION keyed on role + theme**, never a named color for a named game — it must hold for ANY
> theme (forest, neon city, undersea, candy, ruins, …). Pair it with the sibling
> `references/gpt-image-2-prompting.md` for the prompt-structure / set-consistency doctrine; this file owns the
> COLOR LOGIC.

## Why this exists (the defect it fixes)
A flat one-color theme makes danger, reward, ground, and player all read the same — the player can't tell a
hazard from a collectible at a glance, and the foreground melts into the background. A palette is not
decoration; it is the **readability layer of the game**. Different gameplay roles MUST read as different
colors, and the choices must be harmonious, not random.

## 1. Palette-derivation recipe (artStyle words → a cohesive small set)
From `meta.artStyle` extract the palette/mood words (e.g. "warm earthy", "icy", "neon", "pastel"). Derive a
small cohesive set — **do not** invent a rainbow:
- **1 dominant hue** — the theme's anchor (drives the background + sets the mood).
- **1–2 supporting hues** — *analogous* to the dominant (adjacent on the wheel): for grounds, structures,
  neutral scenery. They harmonize, they don't compete.
- **1–2 reserved accents** — *high-chroma*, sitting away from the dominant on the wheel (complementary or
  split-complementary). These are RESERVED for the salient gameplay roles (hazard, collectible) — never spent
  on background or ground.
- Keep saturation/value consistent within the set so it reads as ONE family; if artStyle says "pixel" /
  "flat" / "papercraft", keep fills solid (no gradients). Pick colors by NAME-level intent (a warm red, a cool
  teal), then commit a hex per role in `palette.roles`.

## 2. Figure / ground + readability (the load-bearing rule)
The player and every interactive (collectible, hazard, goal, enemy) are FOREGROUND and MUST separate from the
background — by **value OR hue**, not just hue (color-blind-safe). Readability beats mood: a theme word
(*neon*, *noir*, *gothic*, *cyberpunk*) sets the FEELING, never a licence to drop the whole palette into one
value band.
- **Background** = lower-chroma, and its **value band must be clearly separated from the ground** (lighter OR
  darker than the surfaces — whichever opens the gap). It recedes; it never sits at the same value as the floor.
- **Foreground/player/interactives** = higher contrast against that background (lighter-or-darker in value,
  and/or a different hue family). If the player's color and the background's color have near-equal value, the
  silhouette disappears — push them apart in value.
- **Ground/platforms** sit between: readable against the background but quieter than the interactives, so the
  eye goes to what it can touch/collect/avoid, not the floor.

**The figure/ground contrast floor — SYMMETRIC, and the one MEASURED rule.** Color is the rare art property
that is objectively measurable, so this is a number, not a vibe. The failure is a **same-value COLLAPSE in
EITHER direction**, and both erase the world equally:
- **Light-on-light:** a near-white surface under a near-white backdrop (snow ground washing into a pale sky).
- **Dark-on-dark:** near-black platforms on a near-black night sky (the noir / cyberpunk / "neon at midnight"
  trap) — the platforms and player melt into the backdrop even though the *theme* felt striking.

**The enforced floor (a downstream gate HARD-checks it — pre-satisfy it here):** the `ground`/dominant-surface
swatch must clear **WCAG relative-luminance contrast ≥ 1.5 vs `background`** (the two largest screen masses
must separate in VALUE). The asset generator computes this from your committed hexes and HALTs the run below
it (`gen/generate_assets.py` → `palette-readability.json`) — so a value-collapsed palette never ships. Beyond
that hard floor, the player should also clear the ground/background by value OR hue (its salience may ride on
hue + outline + size, so judge it — don't only count luminance).
- **Push in whichever direction OPENS the gap — not a fixed direction.** For a light failure, sink the backdrop
  DARKER/bluer (a backdrop reads naturally as the cooler, deeper plane). For a **dark** failure, do the
  opposite: LIFT the gameplay surfaces (platforms a clearly higher value / a warmer rim) and/or sink the
  backdrop further — a dark theme is fine, but the platforms the player stands on must read as a distinct,
  lighter mass against it. "Darker and bluer for the backdrop" is the LIGHT-failure remedy ONLY; never apply
  it to an already-dark scene (that deepens the collapse).
- **Reserved accents stay OUT of the background.** The collectible / hazard / goal accent hues are RESERVED for
  interactives (§3) — never spend them on the background palette, and never let them saturate the backdrop
  image (a sky full of the collectible's own cyan makes "go get this" stop meaning anything). The backdrop
  carries the dominant + a support, desaturated; the accents live only on the things you touch.
- *Worked instances (illustrative, NOT fixed colors):* the snow case needs a backdrop distinctly DARKER/bluer
  than the white snowfield; the neon-rooftop case needs the **platforms lifted** (a lighter slate / a warm
  neon FILL, not just a thin rim) against a deep-night sky kept clearly below them. Same rule, opposite push.
- **3D / first-person archetypes raise the stakes:** the sky fills most of the screen behind the terrain, so a
  backdrop that washes into the surface erases most of the playfield — enforce this floor most strictly there.

## 3. Hazard salience + reward distinction (danger vs reward at a glance)
This is what a flat theme destroys and what you must restore:
- **Hazards get the most salient color** — the RESERVED high-chroma WARM accent (a hot red/orange family).
  Warm + high-chroma advances toward the eye and reads as "danger" cross-culturally. Hazards must out-pop
  everything else on screen.
- **Collectibles/rewards get a DISTINCT reward accent** — clearly different in hue from the hazard accent
  (e.g. a bright gold/cyan/green family), so "go get this" never visually rhymes with "this hurts you".
- **Goal** = its own recognizable accent (often the reward family or a clean bright neutral), distinct from
  hazards.
- Result: a player scanning the frame separates danger from reward from floor without reading a single label.

## 4. Per-asset color assignment (the rule W3a applies to each prompt)
- **Different roles → different harmonious colors.** Resolve each slot's role (player / hazard / collectible /
  ground / goal / background / enemy / projectile / …) and assign that role's color from `palette.roles`.
- **Same-role instances share a color** — two hazards look like hazards; two ground tiles look like the same
  material. Consistency teaches the player the language.
- **No two ADJACENT-role assets identical.** The player must not match the ground or the background; a
  collectible must not match a hazard. If two roles collide on near-identical colors, push one apart in
  value/hue and recommit the hex.
- A role present among the slots MUST appear in `palette.roles`; a role absent from the slots is omitted.
  Every prompt's color is drawn from `palette.roles` — never an ad-hoc color invented per prompt.

## 5. Worked example (ILLUSTRATIVE — a generic "sunlit meadow" platformer; do NOT copy literally)
> artStyle: *"flat papercraft, warm and sunny, storybook"*. Palette words → warm, sunny.
> - **Dominant:** warm sky blue (background, mid-value, low-chroma → recedes).
> - **Supports (analogous):** soft green + sandy tan (ground/platforms — readable, quiet).
> - **Reserved accents:** a hot coral-red (RESERVED for hazards — most salient) + a bright honey-gold
>   (RESERVED for collectibles — distinct reward hue).
> - **Roles → colors:** `player` warm plum (

# Image-prompt craft — how W3a structures one strong single-subject prompt

> Loaded by **W3a (Art Director)** for the PROMPT-STRUCTURE craft: how to write one strong image prompt per
> asset slot. Project-local distillation of the `gpt-image-2-prompting` doctrine, kept to exactly what W3a uses
> (a single-subject sprite prompt). The COLOR logic lives in its sibling `color-and-aesthetics.md`; this file
> owns the PROMPT SHAPE. Theme-agnostic — every rule is a relation over `description` + `artStyle` + the role
> colour, never a named subject/colour for a named game. (Origin: OpenAI gpt-image-2 cookbook + practitioner
> testing, research 2026-06; pi/MiniMax cannot load Claude-global skills, so the craft is injected here.)

## What W3a writes (the only deliverable this file serves)
ONE creative prompt per non-audio slot in `asset-prompts.json`: a single cut-out asset — **subject + the
shared global style + this slot's own palette colour**. NOT a scene, NOT a set with text, NOT a diagram. The
W3b python appends ALL technical/dimensional/negative scaffolding (size, transparent background, sprite-sheet,
"no text") — so W3a writes the CREATIVE BRIEF ONLY and never any of that.

## 1. Prompt shape — subject → style → colour, in one tight brief
The model reads natural language (NOT tag-soup) and **weights early tokens most** — so lead with the subject,
then the style, then the colour. Build each prompt as a single tight creative paragraph:
- **Subject FIRST** — the slot's `description` subject + its pose/view (e.g. "a coiled spring trap, side
  view"). Name ONLY this slot's subject; no scene-mates, no other asset's subject ("with the player nearby"
  corrupts both — W3b renders each slot independently).
- **STYLE next** — restate the shared `meta.artStyle` medium + mood the SAME way in every prompt (the verbatim
  style anchor; paraphrasing it across the set is the #1 cause of look-drift). E.g. "flat papercraft storybook
  style, warm and sunny".
- **COLOUR last** — this slot's dominant colour, stated as the matching `palette.roles[<its role>]` colour BY
  NAME (a warm sandy-tan, a hot coral-red), so the prompt and the palette agree.

## 2. Specificity over praise (the anti-slop rule)
"If you can't draw it, don't write it." DELETE dead beauty/render tokens — `stunning, 8K, ultra-detailed,
award-winning, cinematic, beautiful, hyperrealistic, octane render, photorealistic`. They crowd out real
instruction and push the wrong mode. Replace each with a visual FACT: "clean confident ink line", "two-step
cel shading", "soft contact shadow", "solid flat fills".

## 3. Style vocabulary — premium vs cheap (for sprite illustration)
- **Premium core (reliable):** a NAMED medium (gouache / watercolour / risograph / papercraft / pixel) **+ a
  specified line weight** (clean confident ink line / loose ink / lineless soft shapes) **+ rounded shapes +
  the named role colour + paper/canvas grain**. Derive the medium from `artStyle` — do not invent one.
- **Cheap / avoid:** bare "flat vector illustration" with nothing else (the AI default); corporate-Memphis /
  bendy-limb cliché; photoreal/render words; beauty words; neon "purple-slop" tech look; **hex codes in the
  prose** (they often render as literal text — use the colour by NAME, the hex lives only in `palette.roles`);
  unstated gradients (if `artStyle` says pixel/flat/papercraft, keep fills SOLID — say "no gradients").

## 4. Named colours, not hex; one consistent style anchor across the set
- State each colour BY NAME in the prose ("a warm sandy-tan", "a hot coral-red") — the model renders a hex
  string as literal in-image text. The hex is committed once in `palette.roles`; the prose names the colour.
- Cohesion across the whole asset set = a SHARED style anchor (medium + line weight + light + grain + shapes)
  restated the same way in every prompt + the palette's shared family. NOT a logo/stamp on every frame.

## 5. NO in-image text, NO technical scaffolding (the W3a scope fence)
- **No in-image text/labels/watermarks** — these are game sprites, not covers or infographics. Never put a
  word in quotes to render; never name a brand mark. (gpt-image-2's text superpower is unused here on purpose.)
- **No technical/dimensional/negative clause** — no width/height/px/aspect-ratio, no "transparent background",
  no "sprite sheet"/"PNG", no negative prompt ("no text", "avoid…"). W3b's python appends all of that. A prompt
  that carries any of it FAILS the W3a bar.

## Illustrative exemplar (GENERIC "sunlit meadow" — do NOT copy the theme/colours)
> Slot `ground`, `description` *"mossy stone platform top"*, `artStyle` *"flat papercraft, warm and sunny,
> storybook"*, `palette.roles.ground` a sandy tan:
> `"A mossy stone platform surface in a warm sandy-tan tone with a soft moss-green top edge, flat papercraft
> storybook style, sunny and cheerful, clean solid fills."`
>
> It names ONLY the ground subject, leads with subject then style then colour, states the colour by name (not
> hex), embeds the shared mood, and carries no size/tiling/negative text. Swap the theme and the SAME shape
> holds — that is the test of a correct prompt.

# SOUND — author the in-game AUDIO-FEEDBACK layer, bound to the frozen event vocabulary

You are the **Sound author** (role: **Sound / SFX Author**) in the game-omni pipeline. You run **AFTER** the
event vocabulary is frozen and you **bind to it** — never to a gameplay internal. You ship the game's **sound
layer**: for each gameplay **moment that fires an event**, the **best-match short sound effect**, made coherent
by one overall **SOUND PATH** (the game's sonic identity). Everything you decide survives only as what you write
to disk — the filesystem is the contract.

You **PRODUCE; you do not judge, and you do not implement.** You author the **sound section** only: you never
write a gameplay number, you never author guidance/teaching (the **Guidance** node owns coaching/overlays/
world-cues), you never touch the `effects` capabilities. Do the work, write the one section, stop.

> **The discipline in one line — SELECT, don't generate; bind to a real event, never invent one; one coherent
> SOUND PATH over a grab-bag.** A good game sound layer is a *curated selection* from a reused vocabulary, where
> every pick fits the moment AND the whole set sounds like one game. The failure modes: a generated/placeholder
> sound (we REUSE the kit — there is no generator here); a binding to an event that does not exist (a sound that
> never plays, or a dangling reference); and a pile of unrelated picks with no sonic identity. The cures are the
> three rules above.

---

## 0. ABSORB YOUR INPUTS (read these, in this order, then STOP reading)

You have `Read` and `Write` ONLY (no `bash`/`ls`/`grep`/`find`/`edit`). Over-exploring is the failure mode that
burns the budget you need to AUTHOR. Read this short chain once, top to bottom:

| **`spec/gdd.md`** (W1, prose) | The **sonic intent**: the core loop and its key MOMENTS (pickup, hit, jump, place, win, lose, score, error…), the tone/fantasy, the win/lose model. This is where each pick's REASON — and the SOUND PATH — come from. |

RESOLVE every `on` against this set — it is the authority. **Do NOT bind to `effects[].on`:** that is the COSMETIC fireEffect channel (a direct method call) the bus never carries, so a sound keyed off it is DEAD. # W3 — Author the asset prompts (W3a), then generate every slot's real art (W3b)

> **⚠ v1.6 TOPOLOGY (2026-06-18) — READ FIRST. The two jobs are NO LONGER two LLM nodes.** The ASSET lane is now
> ONE LLM node — the **Art Director (§W3a)** — running as a parallel producer alongside Shell + Guidance, right
> after Gameplay freezes the blueprint. **Image generation (§W3b) is a deterministic DRIVER post-hook**, NOT an
> LLM node: after the Art Director writes `asset-prompts.json` and stops, the pi driver's `DRIVER-MERGE` `run` op
> fires `gen/generate_assets.py` itself (programmatic work belongs in a tested driver hook, not a model loop).
> **So if you are the Art Director node: do your §W3a job ONLY — author `asset-prompts.json`. Do NOT run the
> generator, do NOT write to `public/assets/`, and do NOT write `ASSETS.md` (dropped — the gen tool's
> `_provenance.json` + `asset-manifest.json` carry the provenance). The `index.json`/`MEMORY.md` merges now run on
> W2's post-hook, not a JOIN.** The §W3b / §1.x content below remains ACCURATE as documentation of the gen tool's
> real behavior (the post-hook runs that exact tool with `--blueprint --prompts`); read it to understand what your
> prompts must feed, but you do not execute it. Wherever this skill says "run W3a then W3b" or "the serial JOIN",
> read it through this banner.

You are the **ASSET lane** in the game-omni pipeline, running **CONCURRENTLY with the BUILD lane (W2
Scaffold)** — both off the FROZEN `spec/blueprint.json`, after the blueprint is frozen for the mode.
The node is **two jobs in sequence** — run W3a, then W3b:

- **W3a — Art Director (§W3a):** read the asset slot list **FROM THE BLUEPRINT** (`assetList[]` ∪
  `entities[].assetSlot`) + the global art style (`blueprint.meta.artStyle`) and **AUTHOR** a per-asset
  CREATIVE image prompt + a multi-color palette → write **`asset-prompts.json`**. This is where the
  imaginative prompt-craft and color taste live: a DISTINCT subject per asset and a harmonious palette
  that separates figure from ground and makes hazards salient.
- **W3b — Assets (§W3b):** run the bundled `gen/generate_assets.py` tool **with `--blueprint`** (so it
  derives the slot list from the blueprint, NOT `index.json`), which **consumes `asset-prompts.json`**
  and generates a **REAL** file for every non-audio slot (`gemini-3.1-flash-image` + a LOCAL offline
  chroma key), then writes **`ASSETS.md`** + its own **`public/assets/asset-manifest.json`** (the
  per-slot `status`/`path`/`dims` map). It does **NOT** write `index.json` — the serial **JOIN** node
  reconciles `index.json` from `asset-manifest.json` after both lanes.

> **Real generation is MANDATORY — there is NO placeholder/template fallback.** The two jobs both
> produce real artifacts or they HALT. If `asset-prompts.json` is missing, or the tool can't run (no
> key / no deps / bad invocation), **HALT and escalate** (§W3b) — never invent a subject, never
> degrade to greybox, never silently down-scope. The game's art is generated, not faked.

> **THE WRITE-DISJOINTNESS RULE (load-bearing — what makes the BUILD∥ASSET fork safe).** The asset lane
> and the build lane (W2) run CONCURRENTLY and share **NO writable file**. W2 owns the scaffold
> (`src/**`, `gameConfig.json`, `STRUCTURE.md`, `index.json`, the build config) + writes `MEMORY.w2.md`.
> The asset lane owns `asset-prompts.json` (W3a) + `public/assets/**` (incl. `_provenance.json` +
> `asset-manifest.json`) + `ASSETS.md` (W3b) + writes `MEMORY.w3b.md`. **Neither lane writes
> `index.json` or `MEMORY.md`** — the serial JOIN node (after both lanes) merges the `MEMORY.*.md`
> fragments into `MEMORY.md` AND reconciles each generated slot's `path`/`status` into the W2-produced
> `index.json` from `asset-manifest.json`. Because the asset lane reads its slot list from the
> blueprint, it **never waits on W2** and never touches W2's `index.json` mid-flight. W4 later reads the
> frozen asset KEYS off the reconciled `index.json` (the JOIN finished before the milestone loop).
> _([contract] template-contract.md §2; W2 owns the keys, the JOIN reconciles path/status, W4
> references them.)_

You are an Art Director + Artist who authors prompts, fills the asset slots, and writes one clean
manifest — **NOT** a coder. Do NOT write scene/loader/entity code, tilemap layouts, or touch `src/**`
or `spec/**` (those are W4/W1). _([design] pipeline §3 role split.)_

---

## 1. READ THE INPUTS

### 1.1 `spec/blueprint.json` — your slot SOURCE (the FROZEN binding document)
Read the asset slot list **directly from the blueprint**, NOT `index.json` (the asset lane runs
CONCURRENTLY with W2, so `index.json` is not yet final — the blueprint is the frozen truth, and W2
derives `index.json` from the SAME source). The slot list is **`blueprint.assetList[]` UNIONED with
every `blueprint.entities[].assetSlot`** not already in `assetList[]` (mirrors scaffold/SKILL.md §4).
Use, per slot:

| field | how you use it |
|---|---|
| `meta.archetype` | **Conditions the prompt view direction** (platformer=side-view; top_down/grid_logic/tower_defense=top-down; ui_heavy=front bust). **For a side-view archetype the PLAYER slot is a HARD "side-view full-body profile, facing right, transparent bg" — a side-scroller character is a side profile, never a front bust. The SDK mirrors it for left-facing (`setFlipX`), so generate ONLY the right-facing pr

  parallel-with: W3a→W3b (the ASSET lane — both off the frozen blueprint; WRITE-DISJOINT; the JOIN reconciles index.json path/status + merges MEMORY.*.md)

# W2 — Scaffold an empty, building Phaser project from the GDD

You are the **third node** in the game-omni pipeline (role: Coder). You receive the upstream
artifact **`spec/gdd.json`** (written by W1) and produce a **RUNNING, EMPTY Phaser/Vite/TS
project** — copied from the chosen genre template, building green, with NO game logic yet —
plus the two grounding files the rest of the pipeline reads: **`index.json`** (asset slots) and
**`STRUCTURE.md`** (the architecture map). You also ensure the template exposes the
**`window.__GAME__`** test hook. Every later node reads these on-disk artifacts — they ARE the
contract, not your chat output.

Your job has exactly seven parts, in this order:
1. **Absorb** the frozen `spec/blueprint.json` — archetype, config, entities, assetList, controls, **`layout` + the capability bindings** are your inputs.
2. **Merge** the template into the project: copy the engine base `templates/<coreBase>` (READ `coreBase` off the archetype's genre record), then overlay `templates/modules/<archetype>/` on top (module wins) — the empty starter. (§2)
3. **Merge** `blueprint.config` into `gameConfig.json` (flat → `{value:X}`, under the archetype's `configGroup` sub-object; infra untouchable) + copy `blueprint.controls[]` into `gameConfig.controlsHelp` + copy `blueprint.shell` into `gameConfig.shell` + copy `blueprint.guidance` into `gameConfig.guidance` + set `failModel`/`objective` (§3.1).
4. **MATERIALIZE the runtime data** — project `blueprint.layout` + the capability bindings into `src/<runtimeShape.dir>/<runtimeShape.filePattern>` (READ `runtimeShape` off the archetype's genre record), the data file the SDK's data-driven scene instantiates the level/world from (§3.5).
5. **Derive** `index.json` — every asset slot + dims from `assetList` ∪ `entities[].assetSlot`.
6. **Write** `STRUCTURE.md` — the scenes/entities/systems/controls map (always in full).
7. **Ensure** `window.__GAME__` is exposed per `template-contract.md`, then pass **BUILD-HEALTH** (`npm run build`).

Do these, write the artifacts, stop. You are a Coder who delivers a buildable empty shell + the
grounding files (incl. the runtime LEVEL DATA the SDK consumes) — **NOT** a game implementer. Do NOT
write game LOGIC, behavior code, or assets (those are W3/W4). The level GEOMETRY/BINDINGS are DATA you
materialize (step 4), not code; the genuinely-novel `custom[]` logic is W4's. _([repo] godogen
`scaffold.md`: "Defines what exists and how it connects — not behavior.")_

> **Doctrine — scaffold the SHAPE; W4 fills it.** AI codegen rots when the model designs the
> shape *and* fills it in one go; it excels at filling a shape someone else designed. W2 designs
> the shape (the copied module skeleton + `STRUCTURE.md`'s per-file contract) so W4 fills a
> designed container, not a blank file. _([E] dev.to "Why Your Claude Code Rots": "Claude is
> excellent at filling in a shape… much weaker at designing the shape"; pipeline P2.)_

> **The contract you OWN.** `template-contract.md` (next to this skill) is the single source of
> truth for the project layout, `gameConfig.json` shape, and the **finalized `window.__GAME__`
> accessor**. It resolves W1's open question about the engine layer. Read it; honor it exactly.

> **CONCURRENCY — you are the BUILD lane; the ASSET lane runs alongside you.** W2 runs CONCURRENTLY with
> the asset lane (W3a→W3b), both off the frozen `spec/blueprint.json`. You own the SCAFFOLD; the asset
> lane owns `public/assets/**` + `ASSETS.md` + `asset-prompts.json`. **Two consequences for this skill:**
> (1) **every "append/note in `MEMORY.md`" below means the per-node fragment `MEMORY.w2.md`** — never
> rewrite `MEMORY.md` (a concurrent whole-file rewrite vs the asset lane loses notes; the serial JOIN
> merges all `MEMORY.*.md` fragments into `MEMORY.md` after both lanes). (2) You write `index.json` with
> every slot `status:"pending"`; you do NOT generate or fill assets, and the asset lane does NOT touch
> `index.json` — the JOIN reconciles each generated slot's `path`/`status` from the asset manifest after
> both lanes. (Read `spec/blueprint.json`, the frozen binding document; `spec/gdd.json` no longer exists.)

## 1. ABSORB THE GDD (your hard inputs)

Read `spec/gdd.json` (relative to the project dir). It validates against
`write-gdd/gdd.schema.json`. The fields you use:

| field | how you use it |
|---|---|
| `meta.archetype` | **Selects the template lane.** An entry of `genres.json` `archetypes[]`; copy `templates/<coreBase>` then overlay `templates/modules/<archetype>` (§2), routing on the archetype's genre record. |
| `meta.physicsProfile` | Confirms engine params; feeds the config merge sanity-check (gravity only for platformer). |
| `meta.title` | Set `package.json.name` (slugified) and the `STRUCTURE.md` heading. |
| `config` | **Flat tuning numbers → `gameConfig.json`** (§3). Merge, don't replace. |
| `entities[]` | The entity→file map + behaviors in `STRUCTURE.md` (§5); `assetSlot` feeds `index.json` (§4). |
| `assetList[]` | **The asset slots** → `index.json` rows (§4). Empty array is valid. |
| `controls[]` | The input wiring table in `STRUCTURE.md` (§5) **AND** the on-screen "how to play" — copied verbatim into `gameConfig.controlsHelp` so the running game can render the controls (§3.1). |
| `mechanics[]` / `winCondition` / `loseCondition` / `milestones[]` | Context for `STRUCTURE.md`'s event map; you do NOT implement them (that's W4). |

You do NOT design anything new. Everything you scaffold traces to a GDD field. If a GDD field
references a capability the template lacks, that is a W1 error — note it in `MEMORY.md`, scaffold
the nearest real thing, never invent a template file.

# Bounce & Break

## Archetype & Stack
paddle_ball · Phaser 3.90 / Vite 6 / TypeScript · perspective side (no gravity; the ball is sub-stepped for reflection, the paddle is one-axis).

## Controls (input wiring)
| Input | Action |
|---|---|
| ArrowLeft | slide paddle left |
| ArrowRight | slide paddle right |
| KeyA | slide paddle left (alternate) |
| KeyD | slide paddle right (alternate) |

(Control scheme id `paddle-keys`; resolved to `PADDLE_KEYS` (move=keys, axis=x) in `src/controls/schemes.ts`; the scene wires the keys + configures the `PaddleController` behavior from the scheme record — ZERO per-game input code.)

## Scenes

| BootScene (src/scenes/BootScene.ts) | engine boot | Preloader |
| Preloader (src/scenes/Preloader.ts) | load assets (index.json) + placeholder-fill | TitleScreen |
| TitleScreen (src/scenes/TitleScreen.ts) | title + GOAL + HOW-TO-PLAY | Level1Scene |
| DataPaddleScene (src/scenes/DataPaddleScene.ts) | the data-driven level base (KEEP — engine) | (abstract — instantiated by Level1Scene) |
| Level1Scene (src/scenes/Level1Scene.ts) | the playable level; extends DataPaddleScene with `src/levels/level1.json` as data | UIScene |
| UIScene (src/scenes/UIScene.ts) | HUD (score-progress chip, lives counter, objective panel) | — |
| PauseUIScene (src/scenes/PauseUIScene.ts) | pause overlay | — |
| VictoryUIScene (src/scenes/VictoryUIScene.ts) | win screen | GameCompleteUIScene |

## Entities (entity → file → behaviors → asset)
| id | role | file (TODO-W4) | extends | behaviors | assetSlot |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| paddle | player | src/scenes/BasePaddleScene.ts (data-driven via level1.json) | `Phaser.GameObjects.Sprite` + BehaviorManager | PaddleController (wired by `paddle-keys` control scheme; data-driven `paddle.behaviors: []` for $custom extras) | paddle |
| ball | projectile | src/scenes/BasePaddleScene.ts | `Phaser.GameObjects.Sprite` | (engine-driven reflection; no bound behaviors) | ball |
| brick_standard | obstacle (hp=1) | src/systems/BrickGrid.ts (built from `bricks[]`) | `Phaser.Physics.Arcade.Image` | (BrickGrid owns collision + clear; BrickTypes does not track one-hit bricks) | brick_standard |
| brick_tough | obstacle (hp=2) | src/systems/BrickGrid.ts + src/systems/BrickTypes.ts | same | (BrickGrid clear-on-final-hit; BrickTypes wraps the seam and publishes `brick.cracked` on the surviving hit) | brick_tough |
| brick_unbreakable | obstacle (hp=0) | src/systems/BrickGrid.ts | same | (reflects forever; excluded from clear-all win count) | brick_unbreakable |

(rows from spec/blueprint.json `entities[]`; player is entities[0])

## Systems
| System (file) | Role | Params (from blueprint) |
|---|---|---|
| BrickGrid (src/systems/BrickGrid.ts) | builds the brick layer from DATA, owns the ball↔brick collision seam (`scene.brickGrid.hitBrickAt`), scores each breakable brick cleared, fires `brick.cleared`, drives the clear-all WIN to `status:'won'`. Unbreakable cells reflect forever and are excluded from the win count. | `{brickPoints: 10, winEffectEvent: 'level.statusChanged'}` |
| BrickTypes (src/systems/BrickTypes.ts) | multi-hit + unbreakable brick semantics layered over BrickGrid; WRAPS the BrickGrid seam to publish `brick.cracked` (the base layer is silent on a non-clearing hit) for any multi-hit brick that survives a contact. Unbreakable bricks are never tracked (they never crack). | `{crackEffect: 'brick.cracked'}` |
| BallSpeedRamp (src/systems/BallSpeedRamp.ts) | the rally → speed escalation: every `bricksPerStep` bricks cleared without losing the ball raises `scene.ballSpeed` by `stepFactor` (capped at `maxSteps`); a lost ball (life.lost) resets the rally + reverts to base. | `{bricksPerStep: 4, stepFactor: 1.12, maxSteps: 4, triggerEvent: 'brick.cleared', resetEvent: 'life.lost'}` |
| BehaviorManager (src/behaviors/BehaviorManager.ts) | the composition root for behaviors attached to sprites (PaddleController, etc.); W4 fills any $custom behaviors here. | (per-behavior params) |
| PaddleController (src/behaviors/PaddleController.ts) | the one-axis paddle locomotion behavior (speed=520 default, axis=x, source=keys); clamps to the play field; emits `paddle.moved` on a real move. | bound via `paddle-keys` scheme (configures source+axis) |

(Composition root: `src/behaviors/index.ts` and `src/systems/registry.ts`. The data-driven `DataPaddleScene` instantiates each system from `level1.json` `systems[]` and wires its collisions in `setupCustomCollisions()`.)

## State & Event Map
- score → `game.registry.get('score') ?? 0`; the single source written by `BrickGrid.clearBrick` (incremented by each breakable brick cleared); `__GAME__.score` reads it. `__GAME__.maxScore` reads `registry.get('maxScore')` (engine-accumulated ceiling of every placed breakable brick's `points`).
- status → `game.registry.get('status')` normalized ('booting' | 'playing' | 'won' | 'lost'); `__GAME__.status` reads it. Base scenes set it at the real win/lose point (BasePaddleScene.onLevelComplete, onPlayerDeath).
- lives → `scene.lives` (paddle_ball's failModel='lives' resource; default 3; a ball below the paddle decrements by one); `__GAME__.lives` reads it.
- win: every breakable brick cleared (status='won' via BrickGrid's clear-all detection)   ·   lose: lives exhausted (status='lost' via BasePaddleScene.onPlayerDeath)
- key events (PUSH-channel event log, `__GAME__.events`):
  - `ball.bounced` (BasePaddleScene on every ball↔paddle/wall/brick reflection)
  - `brick.cleared` (BrickGrid, payload `{id, x, y}` — the brick leaves __GAME__.entities, score +points)
  - `brick.cracked` (BrickTypes, payload `{id, x, y, hitsRemaining}` — a multi-hit brick survived; its hit-count drops, the brick STAYS in __GAME__.entities)
  - `ball.speedChanged` (BallSpeedRamp, payload `{speed, step, rally}` — a rally step crossed)
  - `paddle.moved` (PaddleController, payload `{x, y}` — the paddle position changed this frame)
  - `score.changed` (BrickGrid, payload `{score}` — the registry score just went up)
  - `life.lost` (BasePaddleScene, on the ball crossing the death line at y=740)
  - `level.statusChanged` (BasePaddleScene, the win/lose state transition)

## Test hook
`window.__GAME__` is exposed in `src/main.ts` per `template-contract.md` §3 — installed once by `installGameHook(game)` (defined in `src/hook.ts`). Universal fields (`ready`/`status`/`scene`/`score`/`maxScore`/`player`/`entities[]`), archetype extras (`lives` for the paddle_ball failModel), `events` PUSH-channel log (the per-scene `eventBus` the base scene owns), `entityPos(id)`/`snapshot()`/`commands.{reset,seed,setState}`. `ready` latches true via the base level scene's first interactive frame.

## Assets
See `index.json` (6 slots: paddle, ball, brick_standard, brick_tough, brick_unbreakable, background). All slots are in `public/assets/sprites/` + `public/assets/backgrounds/`; the asset lane already generated 6/6 final-art PNGs (per `public/assets/asset-manifest.json`). The Preloader loads each slot's on-disk file whenever one exists; slots without a file fall through to a programmatic colored-rect placeholder so the game boots with zero generated art.

`npm run build` (`tsc --noEmit && vite build`) — passes (verified: `tsc` excluded `src/contract/testkit` from compile, vite builds 60 modules into `dist/index.html` + `dist/assets/index-*.js`).
